skill.name: Apple HIG: Presentation Components
skill.description: Apple Human Interface Guidelines for dialogs, alerts, action sheets, popovers, sheets, and digit entry views. Helps choose the right modal presentation pattern for iOS, iPadOS, macOS, watchOS, and visionOS apps.
skill.summary: Apple HIG guidance for alerts, sheets, popovers, and dialogs
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: All static findings are false positives. The skill contains Apple's Human Interface Guidelines documentation for UI presentation components. The flagged patterns (backticks, URLs, generic English words) are legitimate documentation elements, not security threats.

medium_findings.title: Markdown Code Formatting (False Positive)
medium_findings.description: Static scanner flagged backticks as shell execution. These are markdown code fences used to format API references like `UIAlertController.Style.actionSheet`. No executable code present.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Backticks in markdown documentation are standard code formatting. No shell or Ruby execution context exists.

low_findings.title: Documentation URL References (False Positive)
low_findings.description: Static scanner flagged hardcoded URLs. These are legitimate references to Apple's official developer documentation at developer.apple.com.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: All URLs point to Apple's official developer documentation. No external network calls made by the skill.

low_findings.title: Generic English Words (False Positive)
low_findings.description: Static scanner flagged words like 'encrypt', 'server', 'system', 'command' as security keywords. These are normal terms in Apple's HIG documentation (e.g., 'secure text field', 'server connection', 'system-defined style').
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Words appear in normal documentation context describing UI patterns, not security functionality.

content.value_statement: Get Apple Human Interface Guidelines for alerts, sheets, popovers, and action sheets. Choose the right modal pattern for each platform and avoid common UX mistakes with user-interrupting dialogs.
actual_capabilities: Recommend alert vs sheet vs action sheet based on use case
Provide button ordering and labeling guidelines per Apple HIG
Explain dismiss behavior for each presentation type
Show platform-specific differences (iPhone vs iPad vs Mac vs watchOS)
Suggest alternatives to modals (inline feedback, undo)
limitations: Does not provide working code examples
Does not cover Android or Web platform guidelines
Focused only on presentation components, not other HIG areas

use_cases.title: Choosing confirmation dialog type
use_cases.description: User wants to confirm a destructive action (delete, erase, remove). Skill recommends alert vs action sheet based on platform and provides button styling guidance.
use_cases.target_user: Mobile app developer building iOS apps

use_cases.title: Modal presentation decision
use_cases.description: User asks 'should I use a sheet or a popover?' Skill explains when sheets maintain context better and when popovers work on iPad/Mac.
use_cases.target_user: Cross-platform developer working on Apple platforms

use_cases.title: Reducing alert fatigue
use_cases.description: User complains about too many modals in their app. Skill suggests inline alternatives, undo patterns, and when to avoid interrupting the user.

prompt_templates.title: Basic component guidance
prompt_templates.prompt: Should I use an alert or an action sheet for [use case]?
prompt_templates.scenario: Choosing between alert and action sheet for a specific interaction

prompt_templates.title: Platform-specific behavior
prompt_templates.prompt: How does [component] behave differently on iPhone vs iPad?
prompt_templates.scenario: Understanding platform variations for modal presentations

prompt_templates.title: Button and text guidelines
prompt_templates.prompt: What are the best practices for alert button labels and ordering?
prompt_templates.scenario: Writing clear, actionable button text for dialogs

prompt_templates.title: Alternative patterns
prompt_templates.prompt: What are alternatives to using a modal for [scenario]?
prompt_templates.scenario: Reducing modal usage and improving user flow

output_examples.input: Should I use an alert or action sheet for delete confirmation?
output_examples.output: Use an action sheet on iPhone when there are multiple choices (Delete vs Save Draft). Use an alert when it's a simple confirmation with two buttons (Confirm Delete vs Cancel). The action sheet slides up from the bottom on iPhone, making it clear there are multiple options.

output_examples.input: How do I handle destructive actions in alerts?
output_examples.output: Mark destructive buttons with red text using the destructive role. Place destructive buttons at the top where users are less likely to tap reflexively. Always include a Cancel button. Avoid making the destructive button the default.

output_examples.input: When should I use sheets instead of full-screen modals?
output_examples.output: Use sheets for focused tasks that benefit from maintaining context with the underlying content. Sheets slide in from the edge (iOS) or attach to a window (macOS). Ideal for creating items, editing settings, or multi-step forms where seeing the parent content is helpful.

best_practices: Use alerts sparingly and only for critical situations requiring immediate attention
Provide specific, actionable button labels (Delete, Save) instead of generic OK/Cancel
Always include a Cancel button for destructive action confirmations
anti_patterns: Using alerts for informative messages that could be shown inline
Placing destructive actions as the default button
Using the same component for all modal scenarios without considering alternatives

faq.question: When should I use an alert vs a sheet?
faq.answer: Use alerts for critical interruptions requiring immediate response. Use sheets for focused tasks where maintaining context with the underlying content is helpful.

faq.question: Can I use the same design across iPhone and iPad?
faq.answer: Not always. Action sheets appear as popovers on iPad. Sheets adapt differently. Always test on target platforms.

faq.question: How many buttons should an alert have?
faq.answer: Up to three buttons maximum. Use one button for simple acknowledgment, two for confirm/cancel, three when a destructive option exists.

faq.question: What is the cancel button placement?
faq.answer: Place Cancel at the bottom of action sheets (iOS) or leading side (macOS). On watchOS, place in upper-left corner.

faq.question: Should I use OK or specific button labels?
faq.answer: Always prefer specific labels like Delete, Save, or Confirm. Use OK only for purely informational alerts.

faq.question: How do I make modals accessible?
faq.answer: Ensure voiceover announces the title, message, and all button options. Support keyboard navigation for macOS modals.
